INTRO

Five Parsecs : Bug Hunt is a science fiction miniatures game for solo-gamers, though we have

catered to play with a friend as well.

You will take charge of a “Hunter Squad” tasked with going into decrepit space stations,

abandoned colonies or deep-space wrecks and clearing out any hostile alien lifeforms found.

This is not honorable combat against a foe that believes in self-preservation:

It’s brutal, dirty fighting at close quarters and to the last life-form.

As you play, your pivotal characters will gain in experience and reputation, hopefully earning

promotions and commendations.

Or at least 5 days R&R all paid for.

The game is set in the Unified Space setting shared with Five Parsecs From Home, Five

Parsecs : Salvage Crew and Five Parsecs : Gang Warfare.

All of these titles are compatible and you are able to transfer characters from one campaign

game to another.

The rules are presented in black/white/grey for ease of printing.

We have opted for a single-column layout to aid screen-reading, to support the use of tablet

devices for gaming purposes.

All feedback, questions and suggestions can be directed to the author at

nordicweaselgames@icloud.com

Norms

The rules assume a human player against opponents controlled by the game system.

We call these “The Enemy” or “The Aliens”.

The Enemy will be various alien life forms and are generated randomly by the mission system.

We typically refer to an individual model in the game as a “Figure” or “Character”.

The game proceeds in “Turns” which end when every model on each side has had a chance to

do something.

Explaining the models

Each miniature figure represents a single character in the game world.

You can use any scale of figures you enjoy, though you will need to be able to tell each figure

apart.

Ability scores

Each of our characters is rated in six fundamental ways:

*Reactions

*Speed

*Combat Skill

*Toughness

*Tech

Reactions is used to determine when a character can act during the turn.

Speed is straightforward: How far you can move in tabletop inches.

Combat Skill is added to all attack dice rolls.

Toughness is what enemies have to roll above to damage you.

Tech is added to all Problem Solving dice rolls.

Enemy figures will not have Reaction and Tech scores.

Major and minor characters

Many of the figures you deploy for a mission will be Minor characters.

These will come and go and we do not track their fate between games.

Think of them as the people in the background during a television episode.

They are always doing things and may even prove vital, but the show isn’t about them

specifically.

Major characters are tracked in the campaign game and can earn experience points as the

campaign goes on.

Weapon ratings

Weapons are rated in three ways:

*Range

*Shots

*Damage

Range is the longest distance, in tabletop inches, you may attack from.

Shots is the number of attack dice you get to roll.

Damage is the number added to the damage roll.

Weapons may have additional traits granting special rules, abilities or exceptions.

The mission structure

In the campaign you have have a few Major characters that we follow between missions,

typically 3 or 4.

When assigned a mission, you will be accompanied by assault teams of trained soldiers.

These act as additional fire power but are not “persistent” in the sense that we track their

experience and performance across games.

Rather, each mission sees a new set of faces to accompany you into battle.

Think of it like a television show where you follow a few protagonists, while various other

people come and go in the background.

During the mission you will try to accomplish military objectives while avoiding or defeating

alien forces you encounter.

These aliens initially take the form of Contact markers, representing scanner blips.

Around the battle field, you will come across Tactical Locations that may be of help: Computer

consoles allowing you to close down access tunnels, maps allowing faster access, colonial

defense systems and so forth.

Note that there is no inherent limit to the number of foes you might face.

If you stick around too long, you may well get overrun.

Special thank you’s

To my good friends David Platt and Javier Gonzalez for countless moments of inspiration over

the years.

My wife and occasional co-writer Traci Morrissette.

The various wonderful people who have emailed me with ideas.

A particular thanks to the Nitpick Squad for helping catch the bugs.

Inspiration for this game includes the usual suspects (the Aliens and Starship Troopers films,

the Space Hulk and Space Crusade board games, the Forever War novel and probably a bunch

of B-movies I forgot about)

THE STARS OF THE STORY

Five Parsecs : Bug Hunt is a bit of an unusual game.

Any game with procedural generation has the potential to create an unusual streak of luck (or

disaster).

You might have multiple easy encounters only to be demolished by 3 fights against

overwhelming odds in a row.

Maybe you can’t level up to save your life. Maybe your entire crew ends up dead.

In such situations, you will have to determine what to do as a player.

Some players are going to relish going into an overwhelming fight with one guy, armed with a

handgun, a smarmy attitude and a pack of gum.

Others will feel dejected that the dice just ended their campaign.

How you feel about things depends on your views of gaming, but we are going to present a

few options. You may employ each once per campaign.

Write them on an index card and when each has been used, simply strike it off the list.

If you opt to use these options, they can NEVER be regained and cannot be restored for any

reason in this campaign.

To gain new uses, you have to start completely from scratch.

If this sort of thing feels like it isn’t as grim and gritty as you like, ignore it.

1 Ducked at the right time!

A character that was just removed from the battle field returns unharmed in the place

they occupied or as close as possible.

2 Looked worse than it was!

Ignore a roll on the campaign injury table. Character recovers immediately.

3 Did you ever meet my mate?

Add a new character to your team immediately, even mid-battle (teleport beam).

Place the model within 6” of any table edge, ready to act.

4 Berserk mode!

One character may fire at every visible target in one turn.

5 Time to go!

You may abandon one Mission immediately and instantly with no consequences.

TERRAIN TYPES

Gaming terrain can take a number of forms.

If this sounds overly legalistic, don’t worry: A quick glance at any piece in your collection

should tell you what it is considered to be.

I find it helpful to define these terms up front, since many other game rules rely on this.

You are of course encouraged to create unusual or imaginative terrain types for your specific

gaming situation.

Linear

Any wide feature that a miniature cannot

generally be placed on top of.

Examples include hedgerows, walls, sandbag

barricades and similar.

Area

Features that cover a space on the table and

can have miniatures placed within them.

Examples include forest, bushes and rubble.

Area features may be designated as

Difficult.

Field

Similar to an Area feature but ground level.

Field features may be designated as

Difficult or Impassable.

Examples include mud, water or lava pools.

Individual

A single piece of terrain that a figure cannot

climb.

Examples include barrels, individual trees

and sign posts.

Block

A terrain piece that can be climbed or

otherwise interacted with, but which figures

cannot enter into.

Examples include boulders, sealed shipping

containers or locked-up buildings.

Interior

An enclosed terrain piece that figures can

enter inside.

Examples include buildings, tunnels and

caves.

Interaction

Block and Linear features can be climbed.

Area and Field features can be entered.

The Movement rules chapter discusses how this influences movement.

Lines of sight

When dealing with Block and Individual features, use what is called “true line of sight”:

Lean down and see if the target is visible from a models eye view.

If a line of sight is available, you need to determine if it is Blocked.

Lines of sight are Blocked in the following instances:

1: 3” after crossing the boundary of an Area feature

2: 3” after crossing a Linear, Individual or Block feature

3: 3” into an Area feature, even if the boundary was not crossed.

Note: Ignore terrain features within 1” of the firer for the purposes of 1 and 2 above.

Example:

A figure within 1” of a wall could fire across it with no restriction (as features within 1” are

ignored).

A figure several inches from the wall could only fire across it, if the intended target is within 3”

of the crossing point.

Practically this means when fighting across Linear obstacles, either firer or target needs to be

within 3” of the obstacle.

Cover

A figure is considered to be in cover for combat purposes if any of the following are true:

* Line of sight crossed any terrain feature

* Positioned within an Area feature

* In contact with the corner of a building

As we assume some mobility and survival instinct on behalf of our characters, a figure meeting

these conditions is in cover, even if the miniature figure can otherwise be seen completely

clearly.

THE GAME TURN

Each turn consists of three phases:

*Quick actions

*Alien actions

*Slow actions

To begin the turn, roll a number of D6 equal to the number of your major characters and

combat teams.

Put away all dice that rolled over the highest Reaction score in your force.

Assign each of the remaining dice to one of your major characters or Combat Teams.

Any character assigned a die equal or below their Reaction score will act in the Quick Action

phase.

Characters assigned a die over their Reaction score will act in the Slow Action phase.

Note that regardless of the die rolls, all figures will act each turn.

The Reaction roll determines what phase you act in, not if you act or not.

All Enemies will act in the Alien Action phase.

The turn ends when all figures on both sides have acted.

Actions

During a phase, player figures can act in any order desired.

Alien figures begin with the figures closest to the players table edge, then working their way

back.

When acting, a figure may Move and then perform a Combat action such as firing a weapon

or fighting a Brawl.

A figure may opt to forego either option but cannot perform them in reverse order.

text:
�10

Stunned

If a player figure is Stunned, they may Move OR perform a Combat action but cannot attempt

both.

Stunned Aliens will remain in place and take no actions.

After the figure has acted, remove ONE Stun marker from the figure.

Figures can accumulate multiple Stun markers.

Snap fire

Characters that are eligible to act in the Quick Action phase may opt not to do so.

This allows them to either fire when an Enemy moves or to delay until the Slow Action phase.

If a moving Enemy is Stunned, they lose the ability to take any other actions this turn.

Firing during an Enemy move prevents the firing character from moving this turn.

Characters that do not find an opportunity to fire in the Alien phase can Move OR Fire in the

Slow Action phase, not both.

Aliens do not carry ranged weapons and will not use this option.

COMBAT TEAMS

On most missions you will be accompanied by regular soldiers.

These are Minor Characters (meaning you do not track their experience and injuries in a

campaign) and are formed into Combat Teams.

A Team consists of 2-4 soldiers when deployed and must obey certain rules:

Reactions

Teams are treated as a single Character when rolling for Reactions and every figure will act in

the same phase.

Example:

If you have 2 major characters and 3 teams, you would roll 5 dice for Reactions assigning one

die to each team and character.

Team formation

Your soldiers are highly trained professionals (it says so on the recruitment posters) and can

operate independently, however they perform better when deployed as a team.

If a Team is in Formation, they receive a +1 bonus to their Reactions score.

To be in Formation, select a Team member.

Every other Team member must be within 3” of the selected figure.

This is determined at the start of the turn.

The selected figure can be changed each turn as needs arise.

MOVEMENT

When moving, a figure may move up to their Speed in tabletop inches.

Terrain features do not typically reduce movement speeds but Difficult Terrain will consume

an additional 1” of movement allowance for every full 2” moved in it.

Aliens are not affected by Difficult Terrain and move at full speed through such features.

Difficult Terrain includes forest, swamp, rubble and sand.

Figures cannot move directly through another figures at any time.

Opening doors costs 1” of movement. Climbing through a window or hole costs 2”.

Combat teams

Figures in Combat Teams do not have to move together, they may voluntarily break formation

and they may use different movement options (such as Dashing).

Dash

Characters that do not intend to fire can Dash, allowing them to move an additional 2”.

Aliens will Dash 3” but will Dash only if the additional movement permits them to enter

Brawling combat.

Proximity

If a figure moves into contact with an opposing figure, they engage in a Brawl and cease

moving immediately.

Figures may enter a Brawl when Dashing.

A figure that enters a Brawl cannot fire a weapon this turn.

text:
�13

Moving up and down

Vertical obstacles can be traversed by simply paying the cost in inches.

For example, crossing a 2” tall wall uses 2” of movement allowance.

Figures cannot end a move part-way up a surface or wall.

A descent of 1” or less can be performed for free.

Any longer drop requires the character to either climb down or Jump.

Jumping down more than 1” ends the characters turn.

Jumping does not require any available movement, meaning a character can move to an edge

and jump down.

Jumping down 3” or further inflicts a Damage 0 hit.

Characters can leap over a gap up to 2” wide, counting it as part of their standard movement.

Alien creepy-crawlies

Alien figures can drop down from any distance without damage, though they still end their turn

by falling over 1”.

Aliens can leap over gaps up to 3” wide.

TACTICAL LOCATIONS

During the mission you will encounter Tactical Locations.

These represent sites and situations of potential value to your force:

Computer consoles, pathways that can be sealed off, useful infrastructure, adaptable

equipment and so forth.

By investigating and interacting with these Locations, you can gain an advantage against the

alien hordes.

Table representation

Tactical Locations are marked on the table with any suitable marker or token.

We suggest a 0.5” x 0.5” square.

Investigating

To Investigate a Tactical Location, a figure must finish a move within 3” of the marker without

firing a weapon or engaging in a Brawl.

You can then roll on the table below to determine the exact nature.

Alien figures completely ignore Tactical Locations.

They will not factor them into their movement at any point and cannot interact with them.

Resolution

Roll a D6 on the table below.

To activate the Tactical Location requires a dice roll of 1D6+Tech, scoring a 5 or higher.

A failure to do so causes the location to be marked.

A second failure to activate the same Location will cause it to become unavailable for the

remainder of the game.

Any figure that is within 3” and is not firing or engaging in a Brawl (including the figure that just

Investigated the Location) may attempt to do activate it.

Roll Tactical advantage

1 Distract

2-3 Block

4-5 Neutralize

6 Delay

�15

Distract

When activated, all Aliens within 18” are Distracted and cannot act in the next Alien phase of

the game.

Note that Contact markers are not affected but if a Contact marker is revealed within range,

the Aliens will be affected immediately.

Block

Establish a sphere extending 2” in every direction from the marker.

It is completely impassable to Aliens or Contact markers but can be crossed without any

hindrance by player figures.

At the end of each game turn including the current, roll a D6:

On a roll of a 6, the sphere will disappear at the end of the FOLLOWING turn.

Neutralize

Remove any one Contact marker of your choice.

Delay

For the rest of the current turn and all of the two following turns, no new Contact markers can

be placed on the table.

Simply ignore any rule that would place a new marker.

CONTACTS

Contact markers represent sounds, foot prints and the shrill “bleep bleep” of the teams motion

trackers as the hostile lifeforms are closing in.

You will need some sort of marker or token to represent Contact markers in play.

Board game tokens can be adapted with ease.

A visually pleasing option is to take a generic “alien” figure and paint it in a pale grey.

Contact markers are placed by the Mission Generation rules supplied later in the book,

however we discuss their function in the game here, as they principally connect to the

Movement rules.

Moving Contact markers

All Contact markers are moved in the Alien movement phase.

They will move AFTER all Alien figures have moved.

Begin with the Contact marker closest to a player figure, then work your way backwards.

To move a Contact marker, roll two D6 in separate colors.

The DARKER color represents Aggression while the LIGHTER color represents Chance.

If the Chance die is higher, move the marker that many inches in a random direction.

If the Aggression die is higher, move the marker that many inches towards the nearest player

figure.

If the dice are equal, the Contact marker remains in place.

Place a new Contact marker adjacent to it.

From the next turn onwards, they will each move separately.

A Contact marker will move around terrain features as required.

If it moves off the table edge, it is removed from play.

Spotting Contact markers

Whenever a player figure FINISHES the movement portion of its actions, all Contact markers

are checked for Spotting.

The figure may then fire on any revealed Aliens if possible.

Moving Contact markers are checked for Spotting at the END of each marker moving.

Markers not in cover are Spotted within 6”.

Markers in cover are Spotted within 3”.

Note that line of sight is NOT required to Spot.

Troops carry close range detection devices (and most Aliens make scary hissing sounds in any

event).

Upon Spotting, roll on the Contact table immediately.

All results are applied before continuing with the game.

Note that a newly placed Contact marker can be Spotted immediately upon placement.

Surprise!

Contact markers cannot be fired upon.

“Beep Sir?”

At the start of each game turn, roll D6 equal to the Priority of the mission (from 1 to 6, typically

2-3, the Mission set up rules will explain how this is determined).

For every 6 rolled, place a new Contact marker on the table.

Use a random direction die to point to a random spot on a table edge and place the marker

Check for Spotting after placing the marker.

If the marker is Spotted, all figures within 12” may fire upon the revealed Aliens immediately

and out of sequence.

This only applies to markers placed using the Beep sir? rule.

This bonus shot does not prevent the figures from acting normally during the turn.

Roll Contact Effect

1 Stay frosty Remove the marker.

If this leaves less than 3 markers on the table, put a new marker on

the table edge point furthest from the Spotter.

2 We got

movement all

over the place

Move the marker 1D6” directly away from the Spotter, then place a

Place all Alien figures within 1” of the marker.

ALIEN MOVEMENT

Alien figures move according to relatively straight-forward mechanics.

Order of movement

Alien figures are moved starting with the figure closest to a player figure, then working your

way backwards.

In the event that two figures are equally distant, start on the left side of the table and work your

way right.

In most cases, the exact timing of figure movement won’t be an issue.

Movement direction

Aliens will move at their full Speed towards the nearest opposing figure, attempting to engage

them in Brawling combat.

Proximity is determined by the length of the path required.

Factor in any obstacles that the Alien would have to move around.

Note that due to superior, alien senses, they will be aware of the presence and position of every

humanoid in the area, allowing them to move towards opponents in remote locations or behind

closed doors.

The Aliens will ignore any figure they have no viable path towards, for example due to

impassable terrain.

Decision making

If multiple viable paths exist, the Aliens will opt for the path that provides better protection

from fire without impeding movement speed.

Alien figures will NEVER slow down their advance specifically to take cover.

The objective of the Aliens is always to enter Brawling combat where possible.

They will not use any tactical options except the Dash move, which is employed only if the

additional movement (3” for Aliens) would bring them into close combat.

COMBAT

A figure may fire any single weapon carried as a Combat Action.

Unless within 3” of a visible opponent, you may fire at any visible target.

Figures within 3” of a visible opponent MUST attack an enemy within 3” of them.

Weapons with multiple shots may resolve them one at a time.

If the target is destroyed, you may select another target within 3” of the original.

To hit, roll 1D6, adding the Combat Skill of the firer.

The target number to hit is:

Within 6” and in the open 3+

Within weapon range and in the open 5+

Within weapon range and in cover 6+

If the modified score is equal or above the target number, the shot hits.

Missed shots have no effect.

Rapid fire

Aliens tend to come on in swarms that make tempting targets.

When firing at Aliens, every unmodified 6 on the firing dice allows an additional shot to be

taken against the same target or another figure within 2”.

Keep shooting until the gun barrels glow

When firing, keep a note of how many 1’s are rolled.

Any weapon that rolls two or more natural 1’s during one turn has malfunctioned and is

unusable.

The user can spend a Combat Action repairing the weapon.

Roll 1D6+Tech, attempting to score a 7+.

Area weapons instead roll a single “Ammo die” every time they are used in the game.

On a 1 the figure is out of the ammo type and cannot use the weapon any further.

No roll is required the first time the weapon is used in a game.

�20

Brawling

Characters in physical (base) contact with each other will fight in a Brawl rather than firing.

Characters rolls 1D6, adding their Combat Skill.

Characters with Melee weapons add +1, characters with Pistols add +0.

Characters lacking both must minus 1 from the roll.

Aliens may re-roll the die once if scoring a 1. No modifiers are applied to their roll.

The lower total suffers a hit.

On a draw, both characters take a hit.

If either character scored a 6, they inflict a hit on the opponent.

If either character scored a 1, they take a hit.

Aliens count both of their dice for this purpose, regardless of which die was selected.

Hits from 1’s and 6’s are in addition to any inflicted from the combat itself.

Resolve hits using the highest Damage value of any Melee or Pistol weapon carried.

Characters with no suitable weapon strike with Damage 0.

Aliens strike with a Damage rating of 2.

A character that eliminates their opponent can move 2” in any direction, but cannot enter a

new Brawl.

This move is taken immediately, even during the opponents turn.

Aliens that win a Brawl must always move directly towards the nearest visible opponent, if

any.

Resolving hits

If a character is hit, regardless of the source, roll 1D6 and add the Damage rating of the attack.

If the result equals or exceeds Toughness the character becomes a casualty and is removed

from play.

A roll that is less than Toughness will push the target 1” directly away from the attacker unless

blocked by terrain or other figures.

Mark the figure as Stunned.

A Stunned player character may move OR fire when next active.

Stunned Aliens CANNOT act during their turn.

Remove one Stun marker after the figure has acted.

Characters can accumulate multiple Stun markers.

Overkill option

If you find that it’s too difficult to hit anything, lower all to-hit requirements by 1.

This will substantially speed up the game, though it will also make your characters rather short

lived.

Aim option

As an option, a non-stunned character may forego moving to re-roll 1’s on the attack dice.

Simply pick up the 1’s and roll them again once.

NOWHERE TO RUN

Unlike other Five Parsecs titles, there is no morale system in Bug Hunt.

The Aliens won’t stop coming until you kill them all, while your troopers have nowhere to run

and the bugs don’t take prisoners.

As such, you can rely on your troopers fighting to the bitter end, if need be.

PROBLEM SOLVING

Players inclined towards more fantastical scenarios may end up with all manner of things

happening: Doors that need opening, computers that need hacking and strange alien animals

that need calming.

This section offers a few quick solutions to handle these situations as they arise.

Pick whichever fits your situation best.

This section is completely optional and will not come up in typical play.

It is intended for creative players to toy with.

Performing a test typically prevents the figure from attacking that turn.

Quick test

Determine if the test is Easy or Hard, then roll a D6.

An Easy test is passed on a roll of 3+ while a Hard test is passed on a roll of 5+.

Opposed test

Roll a D6 for each figure with the higher roll prevailing.

On a draw, the action is unresolved this turn.

Wits test

Set a Challenge Rating from 2 to 7.

Roll 1D6 and add the Tech score of the character.

If the result is equal or better, the test is passed.

Risks and fumbles

Rolling a 1 on the die (before modifiers) Stuns the character.

If a test is deemed Risky, rolling a 1 inflicts a Damage 0 hit as well.

The turn sequence

Must fire at closest target if within 3” of enemy.

+1 Melee / -1 No weapon.

Suffer additional hit if rolling 1.

UNUSUAL SITUATIONS

This section covers various unusual situations that can occur during the game.

In most cases you can resolve such cases yourself, but if you prefer a bit of guidance, we’re

here to help.

Entering a Brawl with multiple opponents

In rare cases, it is possible for a moving character to enter a Brawl with two opponents at the

same time, typically if they are very close together.

If so, select which opponent you fight at random.

If you win, fight the other opponent immediately.

Crashing into things

Some rules can force a character to move randomly.

Random moves are not reduced by Difficult terrain.

If the character moves into a Linear, Block or Individual terrain feature, the character ceases

moving.

If more than 1” remained of the random move distance, they become Stunned.

If a character randomly moves into contact with another character, the stationary character is

pushed 1” in a random direction.

Impossible locations

Teleportation and similar can cause a character to end up some place that is impossible, such

as inside a solid obstacle.

If so, the character suffers a Spatial Shift and is moved 2D6” in a random direction.

The character is automatically Stunned when this happens.

If a teleporting character would end up in the exact spot occupied by another character, both

suffer a Spatial Shift.

Note that ending up on a Field terrain piece that is dangerous, such as a pool of acid or a

chasm is completely possible. The character becomes a casualty immediately.

subject: Tabletop Games / Science Fiction / Five Parsecs From Home / Bug Hunt
text:
�27

Equal distance

When determining opponent actions, you may run into two possible options, locations or

targets being equal whether due to value, distance or some other factor.

If so, simply roll a D6 with 1-3 representing one possibility and 4-6 representing the other.

If two combat targets are equal in all other respects, opponents will fire on a character that

fired a weapon last turn over one that didn’t.

Cover is made of people!

If a line of sight is partially obscured by another character from the same side, you may NOT

fire, unless the firing character has at some point been in a fight or argument (by way of

random event) with the obscuring character.

This is NEVER the case for opponents.

If a target is partially obscured by another opponent, you may fire using the Hit Roll required for

a target in cover.

If the shot would have hit a target in the open, it strikes the intervening character instead.

Mind your fire

Characters that attempt to target an area of effect weapon where it would strike allies must roll

a 5+ on 1D6 to do so.

If the firing character has ever been in an argument or fight (by way of random event) with the

character in the target area, they may fire automatically.

Re-rolling dice

Any die can be re-rolled only once, unless the rules explicitly state otherwise.

The new result stands even if it is worse.

These options are intended to be as modular as possible.

CHARACTER CREATION

To create a squad, you need characters.

This chapter will walk you through the process to get your campaign started.

In a typical campaign, you will have 3 Major characters that you will be following.

You could increase that to 4 or even 5 if you wanted to have a slightly easier game.

They each begin with the following profile:

*Reactions 1

*Speed 4

*Combat Skill 0

*Toughness 3

*Tech 0

To generate the story for our characters, we provide 3 tables to generate Origin, Basic

training and Service history.

Roll on each table to establish who the character is.

The Effect column will note bonuses to your profile, typically in half points such as “+0.5

Speed”.

Add these up as normal.

Half points (0.5) are used as the increment of advancement, they are not considered during

game play.

For example a character with a Combat Skill of 1.5 is treated as a Combat Skill of 1 during

game play.

Optionally you may count half point Speed increases on the tabletop.

The Advantages column may provide bonus Experience Points (meaning the character does

not begin at zero XP), Reputation points or Missions Completed for the character.

All Reputation gains are simply added together and means you begin the campaign with a bit

of rep already.

Missions Completed are tallied for each individual character and is not transferrable.

It’s worth noting that some results will be more desirable than others.

Not everybody was born with the same opportunities.

Note that the tables intentionally do not depend on each other.

Feel free to concoct outrageous stories to explain the particular combination of traits your

characters begin with.

Players of other Five Parsecs titles will note that since we’re generating active-duty military

characters, they will tend to lack personal equipment in the field.

Likewise, monetary concerns are not a factor since they are, presumably, receiving a steady

paycheck without much to spend it on.

This serves to make character creation somewhat simpler.

Some Origin results are similar to those in other games, but rewards are slightly different to

account for the type of person who may end up in military service.

THE CAMPAIGN TURN

The game is played in a series of Campaign Turns.

Each such turn may represent a few days, a few weeks or even several months.

Think of a Campaign Turn as an episode in a television show.

Each Campaign Turn you will be given a Mission, which is resolved on the gaming table.

Various other activities will take place as well.

Each Campaign Turn is played by going through the following steps:

*Save up cash.

*Determine special assignments.

*Play out a tabletop battle.

*Determine injuries and recovery.

*Determine reputation.

*Update Missions completed.

*Experience and Leveling.

*Roll for Operational progress.

*Roll for Military Life.

*Decide if you muster out.

*Are you entitled to R&R?

This chapter will go through each step in the order they are accomplished each turn.

Save up cash

While enlisted, there’s not much to spend money on except gambling, booze and agreeable

company.

For each campaign turn, your crew manages to save up 1 Credit worth of pocket change.

This has no effect in the Bug Hunt campaign rules, but if you intend to transfer to another

campaign later on, having a bit of money saved up can come in handy.

Having a single shared bank account is of course a bit of an abstraction, but it’s more desirable

from a players perspective.

If you need to buy anything in a future campaign, simply assume that whoever does the buying

is who had the money to begin with.

A character can only ever take a given course ONCE but can apply as often as you like.

Any character that became a casualty in the game must roll for their injuries.

You may spend a point of Reputation to reduce Sick Bay time by 1 turn (Nano-tech care).

This can be done only once per character per campaign turn.

Reputation can make or break your military career.

We track Reputation in the campaign as an expendable resource, which can be used to obtain

various advantages and benefits.

For each Objective completed in the last mission roll a D6.

If mission Priority was 4+ roll 2D6 extra.

Each die roll of 5-6 awards you one point of Reputation.

Reputation is tracked and accumulates. When points are expended, they are removed.

In reality, your characters Reputation does not diminish as such but as you call in favors, you

reach a limit of what people are willing to let you get away with.

Decide if you muster out

If you did not achieve at least 2 Objectives, roll 2D6.

On a double 1, one of your characters (select at random) is sacked for incompetence and

forced to Muster Out.

Pay 3 points of Reputation to avoid this.

Any character with a Mission Tally of 4 or greater may opt to Muster Out, ending their career.

This is principally of value to players who wish to carry the character over to another Five

Parsecs campaign game.

Characters that Muster Out are replaced with a newly generated character.

When a Character has achieved a Mission Tally of 10 or greater, roll 2D6 for each such

character at this stage.

On a 2-4, the character decides to Muster Out regardless of your intentions.

On a 11-12, the character has gone “bug crazy” and must retire.

They CANNOT be transferred into another campaign at this stage but will live out their

remaining days in a rather cozy mental hospital.

The details of Mustering Out characters you wish to retain in another campaign are covered in

a separate chapter later in the rules.

Are you entitled to R&R?

If you have undertaken 4 missions in the campaign, your crew is entitled to a bit of R&R.

In the future, supplements may give you options to consider while on vacation but for now,

cross off one extra turn in Sick Bay for all characters and add +1 Credit to your bank account.

Make an additional roll on the Military Life table.

Once R&R has been taken, you must play out 4 missions before you are entitled to R&R again.

You can pay 1 Reputation point to take R&R whenever you please (by being assigned to light

duties back at headquarters)

text:
�43

ENCOUNTERS

Encounters are resolved on the table top using miniature figures.

You may bring along any character that is not in Sick Bay due to injuries.

The Encounter system requires a bit of player judgement to use but will generate a mission for

you to undertake.

To set up an Encounter, follow the steps below:

*Set up battle area

*Determine and place objectives

*Determine priority

*Determine force strength

*Place Tactical Locations and initial Contacts

*Set up your troops

Set up battle area

The area you are going to play in can be set up in any manner you like.

It is customary for bug hunting missions to take place inside space ships, colony installations

and similar and if you have access to floor plans (such as modular tiles or board game floors)

you can easily play out such a battle.

Players in 15mm scale can easily take advantage of floor plans intended for 25/28mm figures.

However, the alien hordes certainly don’t keep to building interiors and missions can take place

in colonies, urban areas or even complete wilderness.

A typical table (3x3 feet) should contain 10 decent sized terrain features such as large rocks, a

collection of smaller crates, a small structure, forested area or similar.

If using the Planetary Generator supplement, add 2 additional features on top of what is

suggested in the rules.

Distribute these fairly evenly, then add 5-10 small individual features (rocks, crates, single trees

etc.) in any gaps.

Don’t be afraid to add some strange looking plants and rock formations:

Aquarium decorations are great for providing a fun alien world feel.

filename: Five Parsecs From Home - Bug Hunt - Solo Sci-Fi Campaigns (v1.3).pdf
subject: Tabletop Games / Science Fiction / Five Parsecs From Home / Bug Hunt
text:
�44

Determine and place objectives

Set up three Objective markers on the table.

You can place them anywhere that makes sense for your terrain lay-out, bearing in mind your

starting table edge will be randomly determined.

The Objectives should be at least 8” apart, unless you are playing on a very small space (2x2

feet or less) in which case simply space them out as evenly as possible.

It is strongly suggested that Objectives are placed in areas that seem visually striking:

Ruined buildings, by a bank of computers, at a cross-roads or in a room with machinery

scattered about.

To determine the task at each Objective, roll a D6 on the table below.

Notes and conditions

In some cases, you may end up in a situation where the Mission can be completed without

doing anything (rolling a 1 for all Objective markers for example)

If so, simply hold out until turn 4 and remember to buy your intel officer a beer for getting it

right (this time).

Roll Objective Achieved when

1 Ignore This Objective can be ignored. Remove the marker.

2 Scout This Objective is completed when a figure contacts it.

3 Secure This Objective is completed when no Alien is within 3”.

4 Retrieve This Objective is completed when a figure moves into contact and spends a

Combat Action picking up the Package.

If the figure becomes a casualty, the Package is dropped.

The Objective remains completed as long as the Package is in your

possession.

5 Evac If all other Objectives are completed, move a figure in contact to complete this

Objective.

Combat Action. Roll 1D6+Tech and score a 6+ to complete the Objective.

Determine Priority

Not every Mission is equally important.

Sometimes it’s just a recon sweep in a low-threat sector, sometimes its the heart of the storm.

Roll 2D6, pick the LOWER of the two dice: This is your Priority Rating.

The higher it is, the more resistance you will encounter and the worse things will be.

If desired, you can call on your Reputation to be assigned a higher or lower Priority mission.

Each point of Reputation you wager lets you roll a D6.

Every 6 lets you raise or lower the Priority by 1 point.

Reputation points are only deducted for each die that rolled a 6.

Determine force strength

In addition to your Major characters, you always receive one Fire Team of soldiers (identical to

the Support entry below).

You are given Support points equal to the Priority of the mission.

Each point may be spent rolling for extra troops on the table below.

A 2D6 equal or above the Roll required grants you the troops requested.

A lower score means that type of Support is unavailable and the point is lost.

On a double 1 the option is unavailable but the Support point is retained and you may attempt

to roll for something else instead at a +1 bonus.

Each Support choice can only be attempted requested once.

You may assign each point in turn and roll for the result, before assigning the next.

You may spend a single point of Reputation to receive one extra Support roll.

This can be done after finishing your permitted rolls and may repeat the request for something

you already failed.

Place Tactical Locations and initial Contacts

Tactical Locations

You will need markers to indicate Tactical Locations and they should be readily distinct from

the markers used for Objectives.

Place 6 Tactical Locations on the table, spaced as evenly as possible.

As with Objectives, they should be placed in positions that seem significant, such as in or by

terrain features.

We recommend that Tactical Locations and Objectives should be at least 2” apart but on a

smaller game table, that may prove difficult.

If so, simply do the best job you can with the space you have.

Contacts

Place a Contact marker on each Objective (NOT Tactical Locations) and move it 1D6+3” in a

random direction.

Once this is done, place a Contact marker for every point of Priority in the center of the table.

Move each marker 3D6” in a random direction.

On a smaller table, this can cause a marker to end up off-table. If so, place it at the table edge

instead.

Example:

If the Mission Priority is 2, you would end up with 5 Contact markers in total.

Set up your troops

Randomly select one of the table edges with a suitable dice roll.

Both short and long edges are acceptable.

If your gaming table is round or some other unusual shape without clearly defined shapes, roll

a direction die and use the indicated direction to mark off roughly a quarter of the table edge.

Your force is assumed to land by drop ship just off table.

You may set up anywhere within 3” of the selected table edge, with your forces as compact or

spread out along the edge as you like.

If any Contact markers are within 6” of the selected table edge, they are destroyed by fire from

the drop ship as it lands. Get some!

ENDING THE MISSION

Your goal is to complete as many of your Objectives as possible.

2 or more is generally considered a success.

Once that is done, it’s time to get out of there.

We’re leaving!

At any time, you can request Rescue.

Usually this is done once the mission is completed, but if things go haywire, you may have to

get out of there.

Select which character is making the request.

They must be stationary, not Stunned and cannot take any actions.

Roll 2D6, adding the number of Objectives completed so far.

On a total of 10 or higher, Rescue is approved.

Place a Beacon within 3” of any of your characters.

The drop ship arrives at the END of the following game turn.

Any figure that makes it to within 3” of the Beacon is safely removed from the table.

The ship will leave after hovering for 3 turns.

Anyone who didn’t make it must attempt to call another drop ship by repeating the above

process.

The drop ship will fire at the end of each game turn (including the turn of its arrival) with the

effect of two Light Machine Guns with a Combat Skill of +1.

We’re leaving… on foot

If Rescue isn’t coming, you can always get out on foot.

This allows the team to exfiltrate towards friendly held territory but is substantially more

dangerous.

Any figure may leave the fight by moving off a table edge.

However, after the mission you must roll a D6 for each figure that left this way.

On a roll of a 1-2, they must roll on the Lost Marine column of the injury table.

Pay the piper

If no Objectives were completed, you may face a court martial.

Select the character that was in charge of the mission and roll a D6.

On a 1, you are sentenced and the character will be removed from the campaign.

You can pay 3 points of Reputation to avoid this fate.

MUSTERING OUT

The Campaign rules discusses the options for your character to Muster Out.

Likewise, your character may be kicked out due to a court martial.

Once a character has left the service, they cannot rejoin, though they may be carried over into

a new campaign using one of the other Five Parsecs games.

If your character was not sacked due to a court martial, you will receive Mustering Out

Benefits:

*1 Credit per 3 missions completed on the characters tally.

*Characters with 8 or more missions get to keep their Service Pistol.

*If you achieved 8 or more missions, you also receive the trait Friends in the service.

This can be used once to find a Patron, get rid of an Enemy or avoid an encounter with the

law.

Create a suitable explanation as to how your old friends help you out.

It is only good for one use.

Going AWOL

If you can’t hack it, there’s always the option to go AWOL.

This usually happens during R&R. Simply declare that a character books it.

This voids any Mustering Out benefits but can make for an interesting story.

If you transition to a campaign that uses the Enemy system, you obtain an Enemy

automatically (suitable law enforcement).

COMPETITIVE GAMES

We use the term Competitive to mean two players fighting a battle against each other, rather

than one player against the AI systems.

This is very simple and is handled as explained below.

Bug Hunt teams are not generally deployed against other hostile forces such as sentient alien

incursions or rebels, though such games could be improvised if need be.

An Alien master mind

This is the most straight forward manner.

One player simply handles the Alien hordes.

In such a game, make rolls for each Contact marker as normal but if the Aggression roll is

higher, the Alien player can move the marker in any direction desired, rather than moving

directly towards the enemy.

Revealed Aliens can move as the player desires as well, rather than obeying the AI restrictions.

Fighting a human opponent tends to be more challenging and less predictable.

After the match ends, your opponent selects the character he/she felt did the most to aid your

mission.

That character is awarded +1 XP.

If you complete all objectives, you also roll an extra die to earn Reputation.

FIVE PARSECS INFINITE ADVENTURE

Five Parsecs is a living, breathing world that you can travel within.

Characters created in any one campaign can continue their adventures elsewhere.

Maybe your character goes AWOL and ends up in a Gang Warfare game or you join a Salvage

Crew to lend your skill set.

Story reasons

A campaign switch should fit the story you have been building up in your game.

Usually, it’s pretty easy to come up with an answer. Maybe your character wants to do

something new, maybe they made some shady contacts in the service and want to make some

easy money, maybe they got framed for a crime and washed out.

A fitting story will make your campaign that much more satisfying.

The transfer

If your character Mustered Out, they get the benefits discussed earlier in this book.

You will of course retain all ability scores and other advantages.

You do NOT get to retain your military equipment.

It is up to you if you wish to transfer all of your characters at once, or if you prefer following a

single “main character”.

Typically, your transfer character simply takes one slot in the new crew.

From Home

If you can pay 2 Reputation when you transfer out, you can begin the game knowing a Patron

already (old army buddy).

Characters that went AWOL bring an Enemy with them.

Salvage Crew

If your character wants to start a Salvage team and mustered out after at least 6 missions, they

can get a Veterans Loan for their starship.

This reduces the initial debt by 1D6 Credits.

Gang Warfare

If you went AWOL, you have an Enemy now.

�54

Dual campaigns

If you are especially ambitious, you could have multiple campaigns running side by side.

A few of your squad members might set off to become freelancers in From Home for example.

Characters in the Bug Hunt service cannot generally transfer items to or from that campaign,

unlike characters in other campaigns.

This can be a lot of work but also a ton of fun.

Your responsibility

Ensuring a single campaign game is well balanced is a lot of work.

Ensuring that multiple balance in near-infinite combinations is basically impossible.

As such, you may well find loopholes allowing you to “break” your campaign.

Proceed as you see fit for the style of campaign you would like.

DESIGNER NOTES

With each release the Five Parsecs universe expands.

The only question was which direction.

Science Fiction miniatures gaming tends to derive from relatively few sources and films like Aliens and

Starship Troopers looms large among those.

A lot of the existing campaign structure could be stripped away, since military personnel have relatively

few freedoms and tend not to get into that much trouble (comparatively speaking anyway).

That meant we could open up some new sub-systems, including mechanics for staying on or mustering

out (inspired a little bit by Traveller) and a Reputation system to represent the inevitable wheeling and

dealing that goes on in any hierarchy.

On the table top, the main addition is that of fire teams: The first step towards squad-based, large scale

Five Parsecs combat.

Teams act as one for reaction purposes and receive a small bonus for sticking together: Something that

felt like a better system at this scale than punishing teams which broke up.

The combat mechanics also saw a few new mechanics: Notably the introduction of Stun weapons (well,

one in any event) and special results when rolling 6’s or 1’s in certain circumstances.

One of the appealing things of fighting alien bugs is that you can run into some pretty large swarms and

there’s a visceral appeal in mowing down the horde with your high-powered weaponry. The rules should

reflect that and I think they do pretty well.

As always, when doing a focused campaign like this, there’s a balance between trying to do everything

in one book or aiming at a more narrow slice.

I felt the latter would be more interesting: The missions you undertake represent a typical “insertion”

where the team goes in, with relatively little off-table support, handle their mission objectives and then

get the heck out.

You could expand this sort of campaign almost indefinitely: Random tunnel generation, bug holes, alien

queens, off-map support, air strikes and who knows what else.

After much contemplation, it was decided to keep things a little more personal: We are following a few

characters in a campaign after all, and we don’t want them getting lost in the chaos.

There’s always future books for the mega-wars.

The end result is a tighter and more cohesive experience and when you’ve explored what there is to do

here, there’s a whole universe to explore after you muster out.

Peace and love

Ivan Sorensen

BALANCE TWEAKS

Balancing a procedural campaign game is rather tricky: You can play 10 times and still have

combinations of events, items and characters that never came up.

As such, while every effort has been made to ensure things fit together, I am going to enlist the

help of active players to balance things further.

If you have suggestions or concerns regarding any of the following, please contact me with

your situation and any proposed suggestions at: fiveparsecstweaks@icloud.com

Suitable topics include:

*Adjustments to the campaign rules.

*Adjustments to random tables (such as dice ranges for specific results)

*Table entries that need to be adjusted in some fashion.

*Enemies that need to be toned up or down or have their stats or AI adjusted.

*Changes to AI routines for enemies.

*Tweaks to weapons or equipment.

*Game-breaking loopholes or problems.

Please direct rules questions and general conversation to the main Nordic Weasel account at

nordicweaselgames@icloud.com
